# Chapter 4 — Elicitation and Collaboration (BABOK v3)

> Source: **BABOK® Guide v3**, Chapter 4 *Elicitation and Collaboration* (pp. 53–74).
> This is the **central reference** of the `babok-v3-software-requirements` skill. Cleaned, structure-preserving extract.

## Introduction `[BABOK 4, p. 53]`

The Elicitation and Collaboration KA describes the tasks the business analyst performs to **obtain information from stakeholders, confirm the results, and communicate the business analysis information** once it is assembled.

- **Elicitation** = drawing forth or receiving information from stakeholders or other sources. The main path to discovering requirements and design information. May involve talking with stakeholders directly, researching, experimenting, or simply being handed information.
- **Collaboration** = the act of two or more people working together towards a common goal.

Elicitation and collaboration are **never a "phase"** — they are ongoing as long as business analysis work is occurring. They may be **planned** (workshops, experiments, surveys — organised in advance) or **unplanned** ("just in time" conversations). Information from unplanned activity may require deeper exploration through a planned one.

The KA has **5 tasks**:

| Task | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **4.1 Prepare for Elicitation** | Understand the scope of the elicitation activity, select techniques, plan logistics and supporting materials. |
| **4.2 Conduct Elicitation** | Draw out, explore, and identify information relevant to the change. |
| **4.3 Confirm Elicitation Results** | Check the elicited information for accuracy and consistency with other information. |
| **4.4 Communicate Business Analysis Information** | Ensure stakeholders have a shared understanding of the BA information at the right time and in the right format. |
| **4.5 Manage Stakeholder Collaboration** | Encourage stakeholders to work towards a common goal — ongoing across all the above. |

## Core Concept Model in Elicitation and Collaboration `[BABOK 4, p. 55]`

| Core Concept | During elicitation, the BA… |
|---|---|
| **Change** | uses a variety of elicitation techniques to fully identify the characteristics of the change including stakeholder concerns. The change itself may determine the appropriate types and extent of elicitation. |
| **Need** | elicits, confirms, and communicates needs and supporting information. Understanding of needs evolves iteratively. |
| **Solution** | elicits, confirms, and communicates necessary or desired characteristics of proposed solutions. |
| **Stakeholder** | manages collaboration with stakeholders who participate in the BA work. Different roles at different times. |
| **Value** | collaborates with stakeholders to assess the relative value of information provided through elicitation. |
| **Context** | applies elicitation techniques to identify information about the context affecting the change. |

---

## 4.1 Prepare for Elicitation

### 4.1.1 Purpose

Understand the scope of the elicitation activity, select appropriate techniques, and plan (or procure) supporting materials and resources.

### 4.1.4 Elements

#### .1 Understand the Scope of Elicitation

To choose the type of business analysis information to discover and the techniques to use, consider:

- business domain;
- overall corporate culture and environment;
- stakeholder locations;
- stakeholders involved and their group dynamics;
- expected outputs the elicitation activities will feed;
- skills of the BA;
- other elicitation activities planned to complement this one;
- strategy or solution approach;
- scope of the future solution;
- possible sources of the business analysis information that might feed into the activity.

Understanding the scope lets the BA recognise when the activity strays, when people / materials are unavailable, and when the activity is complete.

#### .2 Select Elicitation Techniques

Multiple techniques are usually used in one activity. Selection depends on cost & time constraints, sources of information and access to them, organisational culture, desired outcomes, stakeholder needs / availability / location (co-located vs dispersed). Consider:

- techniques commonly used in similar initiatives;
- techniques specifically suited to the situation;
- the tasks needed to prepare, execute, and complete each technique.

Be ready to **adjust selections** as circumstances change.

#### .3 Set Up Logistics

Per activity, identify:

- goals of the activity;
- participants and their roles;
- scheduled resources (people, rooms, tools);
- locations;
- communication channels;
- techniques;
- languages used by stakeholders (oral and written);
- an agenda (if other stakeholders are involved).

#### .4 Secure Supporting Material

Identify sources of information needed for the activity: people, systems, historical data, materials, documents (existing system docs, relevant business rules, organisational policies, regulations, contracts), draft analysis models. Procure or develop them. If novel tools / equipment / techniques will be used (e.g., experimental elicitation), plan additional preparation.

#### .5 Prepare Stakeholders

May involve:

- educating stakeholders on how the technique works or what information is needed;
- explaining the technique to **non-participants** so they understand the validity and relevance of the elicited information;
- securing **buy-in** from all necessary stakeholders;
- requesting that stakeholders review supporting material **before** the activity to make it as effective as possible;
- sending an agenda in advance.

Eliciting through research or exploration may be a **solo** activity that does not require preparing other stakeholders.

### 4.1.6 Techniques

Brainstorming · Data Mining · Document Analysis · Estimation · Interviews · Mind Mapping · Risk Analysis & Management · Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas.

### 4.1.7 Stakeholders

- **Domain SME** — provides supporting materials and guidance about other information sources; helps arrange research, experiments, and facilitated elicitation.
- **Project Manager** — ensures appropriate people and resources are available.
- **Sponsor** — approves / denies a planned elicitation event; authorises and requires the participation of specific stakeholders.

### 4.1.8 Output

**Elicitation Activity Plan** — logistics, scope, selected techniques, supporting materials, expected output format.

---

## 4.2 Conduct Elicitation

### 4.2.1 Purpose

Draw out, explore, and identify information relevant to the change.

### 4.2.2 Description — three common types

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Collaborative** | Direct interaction with stakeholders; relies on their experience, expertise and judgment. |
| **Research** | Systematically discovering and studying information from materials or sources not directly known by stakeholders. May include data analysis to identify trends or past results. |
| **Experiments** | Controlled tests to identify information that could not be known otherwise — **observational studies, proofs of concept, prototypes**. |

One or more techniques can be combined within the scope of an activity. Stakeholders may collaborate by participating live, or asynchronously by reviewing and giving feedback on documents, systems, models, and interfaces.

### 4.2.4 Elements

#### .1 Guide Elicitation Activity

To keep the activity focused, the BA considers:

- the activity's goals and agenda;
- scope of the change;
- forms of output the activity will generate;
- what other representations the activity results will support;
- how the output integrates into what is already known;
- who provides the information;
- who will use the information;
- how the information will be used.

In the moment, the BA recognises when discussion strays out of scope and decides whether to acknowledge-and-continue, or redirect. The BA also uses this awareness to decide **when elicitation is sufficient and the activity should stop**.

#### .2 Capture Elicitation Outcomes

Elicitation is usually iterative — sessions in parallel or in sequence. Capture outcomes so the information is recorded for later reference and use. If the activity was unplanned, integrate the outcomes into the corresponding planned outcomes.

### 4.2.6 Techniques

Benchmarking & Market Analysis · Brainstorming · Business Rules Analysis · Collaborative Games · Concept Modelling · Data Mining · Data Modelling · Document Analysis · Focus Groups · Interface Analysis · Interviews · Mind Mapping · Observation · Process Analysis · Process Modelling · Prototyping · Survey or Questionnaire · Workshops.

### 4.2.7 Stakeholders

- **Customer** — provides valuable BA information during elicitation.
- **Domain SME** — has expertise; often guides the BA and helps arrange research / experiments.
- **End User** — user of existing and future solutions, should participate.
- **Implementation SME** — can ask clarifying questions and offer alternatives.
- **Sponsor** — authorises and ensures participation.
- **Any stakeholder** with relevant knowledge can participate.

### 4.2.8 Output

**Elicitation Results (unconfirmed)** — captured information in a format specific to the activity.

---

## 4.3 Confirm Elicitation Results

### 4.3.1 Purpose

Check the gathered information for **accuracy and consistency** with other information.

### 4.3.2 Description

Confirmation identifies and resolves problems **before resources are committed to using the information**. Reviews may discover errors, omissions, conflicts, and ambiguity. Results are compared against their source and against other elicitation results. Stakeholder collaboration may be needed to validate captured inputs and ensure agreement with results of non-facilitated elicitation. If results are inconsistent, additional elicitation may be needed.

Confirmation is **less rigorous and less formal** than later analysis-time validation.

### 4.3.4 Elements

#### .1 Compare Elicitation Results Against Source Information

Follow-up meetings let stakeholders correct results. Stakeholders may also confirm independently.

#### .2 Compare Elicitation Results Against Other Elicitation Results

Compare results from multiple elicitation activities to confirm consistency. Identify variations and resolve them collaboratively. May compare against historical data. Inconsistencies are often surfaced when developing specifications and models.

### 4.3.6 Techniques

Document Analysis · Interviews · Reviews · Workshops.

### 4.3.7 Stakeholders

- **Domain SMEs** — help confirm correctness; help identify omissions, inconsistencies, conflicts; can confirm that the *right* information has been elicited.
- **Any stakeholder** may need to participate in confirmation.

### 4.3.8 Output

**Elicitation Results (confirmed)** — integrated output that the BA and other stakeholders agree correctly reflects captured information; relevant and useful as input to further work.

---

## 4.4 Communicate Business Analysis Information

### 4.4.1 Purpose

Ensure stakeholders have a **shared understanding** of business analysis information.

### 4.4.2 Description

Communication is bi-directional and iterative — it requires determining recipients, content, purpose, context, expected outcomes. **Pushing information out and assuming it was received is not enough.** Engage stakeholders to ensure they understand and gain agreement. Act on disagreements. Change the delivery method if stakeholders are not receiving or understanding it. **Multiple forms** of communication might be required for the same information.

### 4.4.4 Elements

#### .1 Determine Objectives and Format of Communication

Common reasons for packaging information: communicating requirements & designs, early assessment of quality and planning, evaluation of alternatives, formal reviews and approvals, inputs to solution design, compliance with contractual / regulatory obligations, maintenance for reuse.

To decide how to present, the BA asks:

- Who is the audience of the package?
- What will each type of stakeholder understand and need from the communication?
- What is each stakeholder's preferred style of communication or learning?
- What information is important to communicate?
- Are presentation and format appropriate for this audience?
- How does the package support other activities?
- Are there regulatory / contractual constraints to conform to?

Possible package forms:

- **Formal Documentation** — template-based; long-term, stable, reusable record.
- **Informal Documentation** — text / diagrams / matrices used during the change but not part of a formal organisational process.
- **Presentations** — high-level overview to support understanding of goals and decision making.

#### .2 Communicate Business Analysis Package

Common platforms:

- **Group collaboration** — communicate to a group simultaneously; allows immediate discussion.
- **Individual collaboration** — one stakeholder at a time; useful when group is not feasible.
- **E-mail or other non-verbal methods** — when the information has a high maturity level and needs little verbal explanation.

### 4.4.6 Techniques

Interviews · Reviews · Workshops.

### 4.4.7 Stakeholders

- **End User**, **Customer** — communicated with frequently.
- **Domain SME** — needs to understand the information to confirm and validate it throughout the change.
- **Implementation SME** — needs to understand requirements and designs for implementation.
- **Tester** — needs to understand requirements and designs for testing.
- **Any stakeholder** will need communication at some point.

### 4.4.8 Output

**Business Analysis Information (communicated)** — target stakeholders have reached an understanding of its content and implications.

---

## 4.5 Manage Stakeholder Collaboration

### 4.5.1 Purpose

Encourage stakeholders to work towards a common goal.

### 4.5.2 Description

Stakeholders hold various degrees of influence and authority over the approval of work products, and are an important source of needs, constraints, and assumptions. As work progresses, the BA identifies stakeholders, confirms their roles, and communicates so that the **right stakeholders participate at the right times and in the appropriate roles**.

New stakeholders may be identified at any point — analyse role, influence, and relationship to the initiative for each. Roles, responsibilities, influence, attitude, and authority **change over time**.

Poor relationships can cause: failure to provide quality information, strong negative reactions, resistance to change, lack of support, BA information being ignored. Strong, positive, trust-based relationships modify these effects.

### 4.5.4 Elements

#### .1 Gain Agreement on Commitments

Identify and agree time and resource commitments as early as possible. Communicate formally or informally, but with explicit understanding of expectations and desired outcomes. **Effective negotiation, communication, and conflict resolution skills** are critical (see Underlying Competencies §9.5).

#### .2 Monitor Stakeholder Engagement

Monitor participation and performance to ensure:

- the right SMEs and stakeholders are participating effectively;
- attitudes and interest are stable or improving;
- elicitation results are confirmed in a timely manner;
- agreements and commitments are maintained.

Continually monitor for risks:

- stakeholders being diverted to other work;
- elicitation activities not providing the required quality of BA information;
- delayed approvals.

#### .3 Collaboration

Stakeholders are more likely to support change when the BA collaborates with them and encourages free flow of information, ideas, and innovations. Genuine engagement requires that stakeholders **feel heard, that their opinions matter, and that their contributions are recognised**. Collaboration involves **regular, frequent, bi-directional communication**.

### 4.5.6 Techniques

Collaborative Games · Lessons Learned · Risk Analysis & Management · Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas.

### 4.5.8 Output

**Stakeholder Engagement** — willingness from stakeholders to engage in business analysis activities and interact with the BA when necessary.
